narrative: Dear [Student's Name], imagine the admissions team looking at your application not just as a stack of numbers, but as the opening chapters of your story toward becoming a lawyer—and unfortunately, the patterns they've seen in stories like yours led them to predict a real risk of struggling with the bar exam, around a 49% chance of not passing on the first try. What stood out most was your Year 3 decile of 7, which was the strongest signal pulling toward that concern, suggesting that even after building momentum, your grades placed you solidly in the middle of your peers rather than rising to the top where bar success stories often begin. Your Year 1 decile of 7 echoed that same mid-pack position early on, reinforcing the model's worry that academic performance might not accelerate enough to conquer the bar's demands. Coming from an upper-middle family income added another layer, as the model has learned this bracket sometimes correlates with added pressures that make the intense law school grind tougher to master. Your LSAT score of 40 didn't provide the boost needed to offset those trends, tilting things further toward caution. Even with your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.3 and being a white female applicant—where being female actually nudged the outlook slightly more positive—those couldn't quite counterbalance the bigger picture. Your choice to go part-time also helped a bit, hinting at flexibility that might aid balance, but it wasn't enough to shift the overall narrative. Together, these pieces painted a picture of someone capable and determined, yet facing headwinds that have tripped up others with similar profiles before they reach the bar. It's not a judgment on your potential or heart—many have rewritten their stories by targeting those early deciles with focused strategies—but the team had to weigh the risks carefully for spots that demand high bar passage rates. You've got strengths here to build on, like that UGPA and part-time plan; perhaps channeling energy into LSAT-level prep early could flip the script next time. We're rooting for you to prove this prediction wrong.
